title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Quick Start

Course Schedule - Weekly schedule and course materials
Course Syllabus - Detailed course information and policies
Lectures - All course lectures organized by week
Assessments - Quizzes, exams, and projects
Resources - Textbook, R downloads, and reference materials

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Overview
STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics (4 units) introduces descriptive statistics, probability, random variables, distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, regression, and ANOVA with emphasis on applications in biological and natural resource sciences.
Lecture: Monday, Wednesday, Friday, 11:00–11:50 AM, BSS 166
Lab: Thursday (select one section: 9:00–10:50 AM, 11:00–12:50 PM, or 1:00–2:50 PM), FH 202
General Education: Lower Division GE Area 2 (Quantitative Reasoning)

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Structure
Includes collaborative daily warm-ups, weekly programming labs, weekly quizzes, two midterms, and five portfolio ready projects. Optional final exam may replace one midterm grade.

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Instructor Information
Instructor: Dr. Rosanna Overholser
Office: BSS 334
Email: rho3@humboldt.edu
Office Hours: Mondays 2-3pm, Wednesdays 12-12:50pm, Fridays 10-10:50am, or by appointment

title: Quiz 8 — Solutions
text:
Solutions for Lab 7 and Lectures 18-21 topics: confidence intervals, FPC, sampling frame, bias, SRS, and interpretation.





Solution: The population is the full group we want to learn about (the target group in the research question).




Solution: The sample is the subset of population units we actually observe and collect data from.




Solution: A parameter is a numerical summary of the population (usually unknown), such as a population mean \(\mu\) or population proportion \(p\).




Solution: A statistic is a numerical summary computed from the sample (known once data are collected), such as \(\bar{x}\) or \(\hat{p}\).




Solution: The sampling frame is the list (or selection mechanism) used to choose the sample. It should closely match the population to avoid undercoverage.




Solution: Bias is a systematic tendency for an estimate to be too high or too low for the true parameter because of how data were sampled or measured.




Solution: A 95% confidence level means that if we repeatedly sampled in the same way and built intervals the same way, about 95% of those intervals would contain the true parameter.




Solution: A confidence interval is a range of plausible values for a population parameter, built from sample data using a statistic \(\pm\) margin of error.







Population: All undergraduates of interest.
Sample: The 120 sampled undergraduates.
Parameter: True population proportion who work at least 10 hours/week (\(p\)).
Statistic: \(\hat{p} = 46/120 = 0.383\) (about 38.3%).




Population: All 300 classrooms in the district.
Sample: The 30 sampled classrooms.
Parameter: Population mean number of chairs per classroom (\(\mu\)).
Statistic: Sample mean \(\bar{x} = 29.8\).




Population: All current clinic patients of interest.
Sample: The 80 sampled patients.
Parameter: True population proportion very satisfied (\(p\)).
Statistic: \(\hat{p} = 52/80 = 0.65\).




Population: All STAT 109 students of interest.
Sample: The 25 sampled students.
Parameter: Population mean minutes/day on course websites (\(\mu\)).
Statistic: \(\bar{x} = 18.4\) minutes.







Classification: Convenience.
Bias likely present? Yes - students at that location/time may differ from all students.
Generalizability: Limited; likely not representative of the full student population.




Classification: SRS.
Bias likely present? Low, if the registrar list matches the target population well and response rates are acceptable.
Generalizability: Reasonable to generalize to the population covered by that registrar list.




Classification: Convenience.
Bias likely present? Yes - club attendees likely differ from all undergraduates.
Generalizability: Poor for campus-wide inference.




Classification: SRS from an incomplete frame.
Bias likely present? Yes - undercoverage bias (undeclared students excluded).
Generalizability: Can generalize only to students in the frame, not all students.








Build a full list of all 210 STAT 109 students and assign IDs 1-210.

Use a random-number generator to select 30 unique IDs (without replacement).

Locate those selected students and collect weekly study-hour data from them.








Mean or proportion? Proportion.
FPC? No FPC (very large statewide population).
Conditions? Likely yes for Wald: SRS + large counts likely met (\(n=220\), \(x=82\), \(n-x=138\)).




Mean or proportion? Mean.
FPC? Yes (finite \(N=300\), SRS without replacement, \(n/N=0.10\)).
Conditions? Reasonable if sampling is random and data are roughly normal or sample size is moderate/large.




Mean or proportion? Proportion.
FPC? Yes (\(N=80\), \(n=50\), large sampling fraction).
Conditions? SRS/independence from design is plausible; normal large-count condition should be checked from observed counts.




Mean or proportion? Proportion.
FPC? No FPC (large campus, finite correction likely negligible).
Conditions? Large-count condition fails (\(x=2\), \(n-x=16\); at least one count < 10), so Wald normal CI is questionable.





Use the class template pattern:
I am 95% confident that the [parameter] in the entire population is between [L] and [U]. In this context, confidence means that if data from many samples were collected the same way, then 95% of the intervals computed from these samples would contain the true parameter.


Solution: I am 95% confident that the true proportion of all undergraduates who are first-generation students is between 0.22 and 0.30. In this context, confidence means that if data from many samples were collected the same way, then 95% of the intervals computed from these samples would contain the true value of that population proportion.




Solution: I am 95% confident that the true population mean time spent on course websites is between 41 and 55 minutes per day. In this context, confidence means that if data from many samples were collected the same way, then 95% of the intervals computed from these samples would contain the true value of that population mean.




Solution: I am 95% confident that the true proportion of schools older than 50 years in that district is between 0.14 and 0.27. In this context, confidence means that if data from many samples were collected the same way, then 95% of the intervals computed from these samples would contain the true value of that population proportion.




Best answer: B is the correct frequentist interpretation.
Why A is wrong: In this framework, the fixed true parameter is not random after data are observed.
Why C is wrong: A confidence interval is about a population parameter, not the fraction of individual values.





Use the summary table to choose the correct interval family, then check conditions before interpreting.


A researcher wants to estimate the proportion of CA high school students who report having a part-time job.



Type: Proportion CI.
95% CI from output: approximately [0.300, 0.428].
Interpretation: I am 95% confident that the true proportion of CA high school students who report having a part-time job is between about 30.0% and 42.8%. In this context, confidence means that if data from many samples were collected the same way, then 95% of the intervals computed from these samples would contain the true value of that population proportion.




An instructor wants to estimate the mean number of minutes per day students spend on the course website.



Type: Mean CI.
95% CI from output: approximately [14.6, 22.0] minutes.
Interpretation: I am 95% confident that the true mean minutes/day students spend on the course website is between about 14.6 and 22.0 minutes. In this context, confidence means that if data from many samples were collected the same way, then 95% of the intervals computed from these samples would contain the true value of that population mean.

End of Quiz 8 solutions.

text:
Mean or proportion? Proportion.
FPC? No FPC (very large statewide population).
Conditions? Likely yes for Wald: SRS + large counts likely met (\(n=220\), \(x=82\), \(n-x=138\)).

FPC? Yes (finite \(N=300\), SRS without replacement, \(n/N=0.10\)).

FPC? Yes (\(N=80\), \(n=50\), large sampling fraction).
Conditions? SRS/independence from design is plausible; normal large-count condition should be checked from observed counts.




Mean or proportion? Proportion.
FPC? No FPC (large campus, finite correction likely negligible).
Conditions? Large-count condition fails (\(x=2\), \(n-x=16\); at least one count < 10), so Wald normal CI is questionable.
